summary: Gruber covers Apple's formal response to the Siri privacy grading controversy, in which Apple admitted it hadn't lived up to its ideals and announced concrete changes. Apple will no longer retain audio recordings by default, will make participation opt-in only, and will restrict listening to Apple employees. Gruber highlights an important clarification that Siri used random per-device identifiers rather than Apple IDs, correcting a misleading impression from earlier reporting. He praises the response as thorough and transparent, with a clear FAQ written in plain language. While his earlier criticism was severe, he views the situation as a genuine series of mistakes rather than evidence of hypocrisy in Apple's privacy stance.
key_points: Apple announced three key changes: no longer retaining audio recordings by default, making participation opt-in with easy opt-out, and restricting audio review to Apple employees only
Gruber clarifies that Siri's 'identifying information' was a random per-device identifier not tied to Apple IDs, correcting the impression left by earlier Verge reporting
Apple published a clear FAQ and issued a direct apology, which Gruber sees as owning the mistake head-on
Gruber maintains his earlier severe criticism was warranted but views the errors as genuine mistakes, not evidence that Apple's privacy stance is hypocritical
money_quotes: This is all good. Opt-in only, with an easy way to opt back out.

The Verge wasn't wrong there, but it's an important clarification that the 'identifying information' was a random per-device identifier that was designed not to be able to be tied back to an Apple ID or phone number.

Basically, Apple is admitting they fucked up on this grading thing, they're owning up to it, and are committed to doing everything they should have been doing all along to protect users' privacy and make everything as clear as possible to users.

My take on this saga was severely critical, but I am convinced this was a mistake — really, a series of mistakes — on Apple's part, not an indication that the company's privacy stance is hypocritical or merely bullshit marketing hype.
It is therefore not surprising, but satisfying nonetheless, to see Apple address it head-on like this.
key_insight: Apple's response to the Siri grading controversy — making recording opt-in, deleting inadvertent triggers, and issuing a clear apology — demonstrates that the privacy failures were genuine mistakes rather than evidence of a hollow privacy stance.
